narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, you've shared a solid profile with an upper-middle-class family background, a full-time study commitment, an LSAT score of 36—which is close to the typical 36.7 average—and an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, just slightly below the usual 3.21. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 66.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales toward rejecting your application to prioritize candidates with stronger projected outcomes. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the average decile of about 5.5—which strongly signaled to the model a risk of struggling on the high-stakes bar. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 4, again underperforming the typical 5.7 average and reinforcing concerns about consistent academic strength early on. On a more positive note, your LSAT score pushed back against failure, as it held up well against the dataset average and suggested solid reasoning skills that could help you pass. Compared to typical students who get approved, your lower decile rankings in key undergrad years stand out as the main departure, while your other metrics align more closely with the pack. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male from a supportive background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements—but the model weighs these academic signals heavily for bar passage. To move forward, focus on bolstering your recent grades through retakes, supplemental courses, or tutoring to climb those decile rankings, as even small gains there could flip future predictions. Consider gaining practical experience like internships to round out your profile, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends. You're close in several areas, so persistence with a clear plan could open those doors soon. Keep building on your LSAT strength, and don't hesitate to seek advice from law school advisors familiar with paths for students like you.
